clinicalStem: A 9-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother after 3 days of watery diarrhea and vomiting. On examination he is lethargic and does not cry when blood is drawn. His eyes are sunken, his skin turgor is markedly reduced with a skin pinch returning in more than 2 seconds, and his mucous membranes are dry. His weight today is 6.5 kg; his mother reports his weight one week ago was 7.4 kg. HR is 178 bpm, BP is 68/40 mmHg, capillary refill is 4 seconds, and his extremities are cold and mottled. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate immediate management?
options: Begin oral rehydration therapy (ORT) with low-osmolarity ORS at 20 mL/kg over 4 hours
Administer IV 0.9% normal saline 20 mL/kg bolus as fast as possible and reassess
Insert an NG tube and start rapid nasogastric rehydration at 20 mL/kg/hour
Start maintenance IV fluids at 100 mL/kg over 24 hours and monitor urine output
Obtain blood cultures, start IV antibiotics, and defer fluid resuscitation until cultures are drawn
explanation: This infant has severe dehydration with signs of hypovolemic shock — lethargy, cold mottled extremities, prolonged capillary refill, tachycardia, and hypotension. The WHO and IMCI protocol mandates immediate IV fluid resuscitation with isotonic saline 20 mL/kg bolus as rapidly as possible, repeated until perfusion improves. Oral or nasogastric rehydration is contraindicated in a child with altered consciousness or circulatory compromise. Antibiotics and cultures are secondary priorities once the airway and circulation are stabilised.
wrongAnswerExplanations: ORT is appropriate for mild-to-moderate dehydration without signs of shock; this child is in circulatory failure and cannot safely absorb enteral fluids.
Correct — IV isotonic saline bolus at 20 mL/kg as fast as possible is the first-line treatment for a child in hypovolemic shock.
NG rehydration is an alternative for moderate dehydration when IV access fails; it is not appropriate for a child in frank shock with impaired consciousness.
Maintenance fluid rates are far too slow for a child in shock; rapid volume resuscitation is needed first before any maintenance calculation.
Deferring resuscitation to draw cultures is dangerous and delays life-saving treatment; fluid resuscitation takes absolute priority in hypovolemic shock.
clinicalPearl: In a child with severe dehydration and signs of shock, IV isotonic saline 20 mL/kg bolus given as fast as possible is the immediate priority — oral rehydration is absolutely contraindicated.
keyClue: Lethargy, capillary refill 4 seconds, cold mottled extremities, and hypotension indicate hypovolemic shock requiring emergency IV resuscitation.
commonExamTrap: Choosing ORT or nasogastric rehydration because diarrhea is the primary diagnosis — in shock, the route and urgency of rehydration overrides the usual diarrhea protocol.
missedConcept: Failure to recognise the clinical threshold at which dehydration becomes haemodynamic shock, requiring IV bolus resuscitation rather than oral rehydration.
redFlagReason: Child is in hypovolemic shock (hypotension, prolonged CRT, lethargy). Delayed or inadequate resuscitation can lead to cardiac arrest and death.

clinicalStem: A 2-year-old girl presents with a 2-day history of diarrhea (8 loose stools per day) and one episode of vomiting. She is irritable and cries when examined. Her eyes appear mildly sunken and she drinks eagerly when offered water. Skin turgor is mildly reduced — the skin pinch returns to normal in just under 2 seconds. Her mucous membranes are slightly dry. HR is 128 bpm, capillary refill is 2 seconds, and she is afebrile. Which dehydration classification best describes this child and what is the appropriate initial management?
options: No dehydration — continue normal feeding and counsel on home ORS use
Some dehydration — administer ORS 75 mL/kg over 4 hours and reassess
Severe dehydration — start IV normal saline 20 mL/kg bolus immediately
Some dehydration — admit for 24-hour IV maintenance fluids
No dehydration — discharge immediately with no specific intervention
explanation: This child has two or more signs of dehydration (sunken eyes, reduced skin turgor, dry mucous membranes, irritability, drinks eagerly) without signs of shock, fitting the WHO classification of 'some dehydration.' The standard WHO/IMCI management is oral rehydration therapy with low-osmolarity ORS at 75 mL/kg over 4 hours, with reassessment at the end. IV fluids are not yet indicated as there is no circulatory compromise.
wrongAnswerExplanations: This child has multiple clinical signs of dehydration; classifying her as 'no dehydration' would undertreat her and risk progression to severe dehydration.
Correct — two or more signs without shock = 'some dehydration'; WHO protocol is ORS 75 mL/kg over 4 hours.
Severe dehydration requires signs of circulatory failure (shock); this child has normal CRT, is not hypotensive, and drinks eagerly — IV bolus is not yet indicated.
IV maintenance fluids are not the first-line treatment for 'some dehydration' when the child can drink and has no circulatory compromise.
Discharging without treatment is dangerous; untreated moderate dehydration can progress to severe dehydration and shock within hours.
clinicalPearl: The WHO sign 'drinks eagerly' combined with sunken eyes and mildly reduced skin turgor classifies a child as 'some dehydration,' treated with supervised ORS 75 mL/kg over 4 hours — not IV fluids.
keyClue: Drinks eagerly, sunken eyes, mildly reduced skin turgor, CRT 2 seconds, and no hypotension — all consistent with 'some' but not 'severe' dehydration.
commonExamTrap: Choosing IV fluids because the child 'looks dehydrated' — IV is reserved for severe dehydration with circulatory compromise or when ORT fails.
missedConcept: The WHO three-tier dehydration classification and the specific ORS dose (75 mL/kg over 4 hours) for the 'some dehydration' category.

clinicalStem: A 9-month-old previously healthy boy is brought to the ED with a 6-hour history of intermittent, severe colicky crying. Between episodes he appears pale and listless. He has vomited twice. On examination, his abdomen is soft between episodes, and a sausage-shaped mass is palpable in the right upper quadrant. Rectal examination reveals currant-jelly stool. An abdominal ultrasound confirms a target sign in the right upper quadrant. He is haemodynamically stable with no signs of peritonism. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: Immediate surgical laparotomy for manual reduction
Air or hydrostatic enema reduction under fluoroscopic or ultrasound guidance
IV morphine for pain control followed by a trial of spontaneous resolution over 12 hours
Nasogastric decompression and broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for 48 hours before reduction
Barium enema followed by surgical review if unsuccessful
explanation: Intussusception in a haemodynamically stable child without signs of peritonitis or bowel perforation is initially managed with non-operative reduction using air or hydrostatic (saline or water-soluble contrast) enema under fluoroscopic or ultrasound guidance. This approach has a success rate of 80–95% and avoids surgery in most cases. Immediate surgery is reserved for peritonitis, perforation, clinical instability, or failed enema reduction. Barium enema is no longer preferred due to the risk of peritoneal soiling if perforation occurs.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Immediate surgery is reserved for children with peritonitis, perforation, or failed non-operative reduction; a stable child with no peritonism should first have enema reduction attempted.
Correct — pneumatic or hydrostatic enema under imaging guidance is the first-line treatment for uncomplicated intussusception in a stable child.
Intussusception is a surgical emergency; spontaneous resolution is rare after 6 hours, and watchful waiting risks bowel ischaemia and perforation.
Antibiotics and NG decompression alone do not reduce the intussusception; this approach wastes critical time and risks bowel necrosis.
Barium enema is avoided because barium spillage into the peritoneum after perforation causes severe peritonitis; air or water-soluble contrast enema is preferred.
clinicalPearl: Currant-jelly stool + sausage-shaped mass + colicky crying in an infant = intussusception; first-line treatment in a stable child is pneumatic or hydrostatic enema — not immediate surgery.
keyClue: Sausage-shaped RUQ mass, currant-jelly stool, and ultrasound target sign confirm intussusception; haemodynamic stability means non-operative reduction is appropriate.
commonExamTrap: Choosing immediate laparotomy — surgery is reserved for failed reduction or complications (perforation, peritonitis), not as initial treatment in a stable child.
missedConcept: Non-operative enema reduction is the correct first-line approach for haemodynamically stable intussusception; surgery is not the default first step.
redFlagReason: Intussusception is a surgical emergency; delayed diagnosis or management leads to bowel ischaemia, perforation, and septic shock.

clinicalStem: A 4-week-old male infant is brought to the clinic with a 10-day history of progressively worsening, projectile, non-bilious vomiting after every feed. He appears hungry and feeds eagerly after vomiting. On examination he is moderately dehydrated, and a firm, olive-shaped mass is palpable in the epigastrium. Ultrasound confirms pyloric channel length of 18 mm and wall thickness of 4 mm. Blood gas shows pH 7.54, chloride 88 mEq/L, sodium 132 mEq/L, and potassium 3.0 mEq/L. Which is the MOST appropriate immediate management priority?
options: Emergency surgical pyloromyotomy within the next 2 hours
IV fluid resuscitation with normal saline containing potassium chloride to correct the metabolic alkalosis before surgery
Trial of oral atropine sulphate to relax the pylorus and defer surgery
Immediate nasogastric feeding with high-calorie formula to correct malnutrition before surgery
Upper GI contrast study to confirm the diagnosis before any intervention
explanation: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis causes repeated vomiting of gastric contents, leading to hypochloraemic, hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis. Surgery (Ramstedt pyloromyotomy) is the definitive treatment, but it is NOT an emergency — operating on a child with uncorrected metabolic alkalosis risks post-anaesthetic apnoea from persistent alkalosis affecting respiratory drive. The immediate priority is IV rehydration with normal saline and potassium supplementation to normalise electrolytes and pH before the elective pyloromyotomy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pyloric stenosis is not a surgical emergency requiring immediate operation; operating before correcting the metabolic alkalosis risks anaesthetic complications including post-operative apnoea.
Correct — the immediate priority is correcting the hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis with IV saline + KCl; pyloromyotomy is then performed as an urgent (not emergency) procedure.
Oral atropine can be used in select cases but is not the standard of care; it does not address the acute electrolyte derangement and is not appropriate in a significantly dehydrated infant.
NG feeding does not correct the alkalosis and the thickened pylorus will prevent gastric emptying; it does not substitute for the necessary surgical correction.
Ultrasound already confirms the diagnosis (pyloric length ≥14 mm, thickness ≥4 mm); a contrast study is unnecessary and delays treatment.
clinicalPearl: Pyloric stenosis = surgical diagnosis but medical emergency first — correct the hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis with IV normal saline + KCl before taking the baby to theatre.
keyClue: pH 7.54, low chloride, low potassium in a vomiting 4-week-old with an olive mass = pyloric stenosis with metabolic alkalosis requiring electrolyte correction before surgery.
commonExamTrap: Choosing emergency immediate surgery — pyloric stenosis is urgent but NOT an emergency; metabolic correction must precede anaesthesia.
missedConcept: Uncorrected metabolic alkalosis in pyloric stenosis causes post-anaesthetic respiratory depression; pre-operative electrolyte normalisation is mandatory before pyloromyotomy.

clinicalStem: A 6-week-old male infant is brought by his parents because he has not passed stool for 8 days and his abdomen is distended. The parents report he passed his first meconium at 48 hours of life. He has been formula-fed since birth. On examination he is irritable, his abdomen is markedly distended and tympanic, and there is no stool on digital rectal examination, but withdrawal of the examining finger is followed by an explosive release of gas and liquid stool. Abdominal X-ray shows massive colonic dilatation. Which is the MOST appropriate next management step?
options: Increase formula feeds and reassure the parents that constipation is common in formula-fed infants
Start osmotic laxatives and reassess in one week as an outpatient
Urgent surgical referral for rectal suction biopsy and definitive surgical management
Administer a phosphate enema and observe for 24 hours
Start IV antibiotics for presumed necrotising enterocolitis and arrange urgent CT abdomen
explanation: This infant has the classic features of Hirschsprung disease: delayed passage of meconium (>24–48 hours), progressive abdominal distension, failure to pass stool, and explosive decompression on rectal examination. The definitive investigation is rectal suction biopsy showing absence of ganglion cells, and management is surgical pull-through. Urgent surgical referral is essential as these children are at risk of Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (HAEC), which can be life-threatening. Laxatives and reassurance are inappropriate and dangerous in this setting.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Delaying diagnosis with reassurance in a 6-week-old with 8 days of obstruction risks Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis, which carries significant mortality.
Osmotic laxatives do not address the underlying aganglionosis and can precipitate enterocolitis; outpatient deferral is dangerous in a significantly distended infant.
Correct — urgent surgical referral for rectal suction biopsy and pull-through surgery is the appropriate management for suspected Hirschsprung disease.
Phosphate enemas are contraindicated in infants as they can cause hyperphosphataemia and hypocalcaemia; they do not address the underlying pathology.
This presentation is far more consistent with Hirschsprung disease than NEC; CT and antibiotics alone delay the definitive surgical treatment needed.
clinicalPearl: Delayed meconium passage (>48 hrs) + progressive abdominal distension + explosive decompression on PR examination = Hirschsprung disease until proven otherwise; urgent surgical referral is mandatory.
keyClue: Delayed meconium passage (48 hours), no stool for 8 days at 6 weeks of age, and explosive release of gas on PR examination are the hallmark triad of Hirschsprung disease.
commonExamTrap: Attributing constipation in a formula-fed infant to diet — delayed meconium and progressive obstruction mandate exclusion of Hirschsprung disease before any dietary reassurance.
missedConcept: Hirschsprung disease is an organic cause of neonatal/infantile obstruction; laxatives and reassurance are contraindicated and risk fatal Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis.
redFlagReason: Risk of Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (HAEC), which carries significant mortality if the diagnosis is missed and the infant is not urgently referred for surgery.

subtopic: Safe correction to prevent cerebral oedema
clinicalStem: A 3-month-old exclusively breastfed girl is brought to hospital with a 4-day history of diarrhoea and poor feeding. On examination she is irritable but alert; her mucous membranes are dry, her skin has a 'doughy' consistency despite reduced turgor, and her anterior fontanelle is sunken. Serum sodium is 162 mEq/L, glucose is 4.2 mmol/L. She weighs 4.2 kg. She has no signs of circulatory shock. Which rehydration strategy is MOST appropriate?
options: Rapid IV rehydration with 0.9% saline at 20 mL/kg over 1 hour to restore circulating volume
Slow IV rehydration targeting correction of serum sodium by no more than 10–12 mEq/L per 24 hours
Immediate free water (5% dextrose) administration to rapidly lower the serum sodium to normal
Oral rehydration with plain water to replace free water deficit over 2 hours
No IV fluids; resume breastfeeding on demand and reassess sodium in 48 hours
explanation: Hypernatraemic dehydration (Na >150 mEq/L) requires slow correction: the serum sodium should be lowered by no more than 10–12 mEq/L per 24 hours to prevent a rapid osmotic shift of water into brain cells, causing cerebral oedema, seizures, and brain herniation. In the absence of shock, a gradual rehydration approach using hypotonic or isotonic fluids over 48 hours is the standard of care. Rapid or free-water correction risks catastrophic cerebral oedema.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Rapid large-volume saline boluses are reserved for shock; this child has no circulatory compromise, and rapid fluid delivery risks too-rapid sodium correction causing cerebral oedema.
Correct — in hypernatraemia without shock, slow correction at ≤10–12 mEq/L per 24 hours prevents osmotic cerebral oedema.
Giving free water (5% dextrose) rapidly drops serum osmolality and causes acute water shift into brain cells, leading to cerebral oedema and seizures — this is dangerous.
Oral plain water rapidly corrects free-water deficit but is uncontrolled and can drop sodium too quickly in a severely hypernatraemic infant.
An Na of 162 mEq/L with clinical dehydration signs requires active medical management; deferred reassessment without treatment risks progression to seizures and brain injury.
clinicalPearl: Hypernatraemic dehydration: 'doughy skin' is the classic sign; correct serum sodium no faster than 10–12 mEq/L/24 hours — rapid correction causes cerebral oedema and seizures.
keyClue: Serum sodium 162 mEq/L and 'doughy' skin texture are the hallmarks of hypernatraemic dehydration, mandating slow rehydration to prevent cerebral oedema.
commonExamTrap: Using rapid IV bolus or free water to 'fix' the high sodium quickly — rapid correction of hypernatraemia is as dangerous as the hypernatraemia itself.
missedConcept: The brain adapts to chronic hypernatraemia by accumulating idiogenic osmoles; rapid free-water replacement causes acute brain swelling, seizures, and death.
redFlagReason: Rapid correction of hypernatraemic dehydration causes cerebral oedema, seizures, and brain herniation — a potentially fatal iatrogenic complication.

clinicalStem: A 14-month-old boy living in a northern city is brought by his mother for a health check. He is exclusively breastfed. On examination he has frontal bossing, widened wrists, a rachitic rosary, and bowing of the legs. He has not yet started walking. X-ray of the wrists shows cupped and frayed metaphyses. Serum calcium is 1.95 mmol/L, phosphate is 0.8 mmol/L, ALP is 950 IU/L, and 25-OH vitamin D is 12 nmol/L. Which is the MOST appropriate initial management?
options: Recommend increased sun exposure only and reassess in 6 months
Start oral vitamin D supplementation (2000–6000 IU/day) and calcium supplementation, with monitoring of biochemistry
Administer a single IM stoss dose of vitamin D 300,000 IU followed by no further supplementation
Start a high-phosphate diet and refer to orthopaedics for immediate leg-straightening splints
Start IV calcium gluconate as an inpatient and defer vitamin D replacement
explanation: Nutritional rickets in a breastfed infant with low 25-OH vitamin D is treated with oral vitamin D supplementation (typically 2000–6000 IU/day depending on severity and local guidelines) combined with calcium supplementation to prevent hungry-bone syndrome during healing. Biochemical monitoring (calcium, phosphate, ALP, 25-OH-D) guides duration of treatment. Increased sun exposure alone is inadequate in a symptomatic, severe case. IM stoss therapy is a valid alternative in non-adherent patients but is not first-line. IV calcium is reserved for symptomatic hypocalcaemia (tetany, seizures).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Sun exposure alone is insufficient to treat established symptomatic rickets with severe biochemical deficiency; pharmacological supplementation is required.
Correct — oral vitamin D (high-dose treatment range) plus calcium supplementation is the first-line management for nutritional rickets.
IM stoss therapy (single high-dose) is a valid option for non-adherence situations but is not first-line; it also does not address concurrent hypocalcaemia risk without calcium co-supplementation.
Phosphate diet changes are relevant in X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets, not nutritional rickets; orthopaedic splints are not indicated at this stage — biochemical correction comes first.
IV calcium is reserved for acute symptomatic hypocalcaemia (seizures, tetany); this child has no acute symptoms and oral supplementation is safe and preferred.
clinicalPearl: Nutritional rickets in an exclusively breastfed infant: start high-dose oral vitamin D plus calcium supplementation — calcium co-supplementation prevents post-treatment hypocalcaemia (hungry-bone syndrome).
keyClue: Frontal bossing, rachitic rosary, cupped metaphyses, low 25-OH-D 12 nmol/L, and elevated ALP in an exclusively breastfed toddler = nutritional vitamin D deficiency rickets.
commonExamTrap: Choosing sun exposure alone or IV calcium first — sun alone is insufficient for established rickets, and IV calcium is only for symptomatic acute hypocalcaemia.
missedConcept: Calcium supplementation alongside vitamin D is essential in treating rickets to prevent hungry-bone syndrome (severe hypocalcaemia as bones remineralise rapidly during treatment).

subtopic: F-75 vs F-100 formula and complication avoidance
clinicalStem: A 2-year-old girl from a rural area is admitted with severe wasting (weight-for-height Z-score < −3). She is listless and has generalised pitting oedema. She is afebrile with HR 110 bpm and RR 32/min. Her haemoglobin is 6.2 g/dL. There is no obvious source of infection. A nurse suggests starting high-calorie (F-100) therapeutic milk immediately to speed recovery. What is the MOST appropriate initial feeding approach?
options: Start F-100 therapeutic milk at full volume to replenish calories as quickly as possible
Start F-75 starter formula at low volume and advance slowly to avoid refeeding syndrome
Give a single bolus of 50% dextrose IV to correct presumed hypoglycaemia first
Commence nasogastric feeds of standard infant formula at maintenance caloric requirements
Delay all feeding for 48 hours and administer IV TPN while monitoring electrolytes
explanation: Children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) must be started on F-75 (stabilisation formula — 75 kcal/100 mL) in the initial stabilisation phase because their metabolic capacity is severely compromised. Immediately giving high-calorie F-100 risks refeeding syndrome — a dangerous electrolyte shift (especially hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia) triggered by rapid carbohydrate refeeding, causing cardiac failure, arrhythmia, and death. F-75 is given in small, frequent amounts for 1–2 weeks before transitioning to F-100 in the rehabilitation phase. The oedema here indicates kwashiorkor or marasmic kwashiorkor, making careful fluid management critical.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Starting F-100 immediately in the stabilisation phase risks fatal refeeding syndrome; high-calorie feeding is for the rehabilitation phase only.
Correct — F-75 at low volume advancing slowly is the WHO-recommended stabilisation phase approach to prevent refeeding syndrome in SAM.
Routine IV dextrose boluses are not indicated unless documented hypoglycaemia (blood glucose <3 mmol/L) is confirmed; empirical bolus without hypoglycaemia can be harmful.
Standard infant formula is not designed for SAM management; it does not provide the specific nutrient ratios required and does not follow WHO SAM protocols.
Delaying all feeding increases catabolism, worsens hypoglycaemia, and worsens outcomes; early (cautious) enteral feeding is recommended by WHO guidelines.
clinicalPearl: SAM initial management: ALWAYS start F-75 (not F-100) in the stabilisation phase — giving high-calorie formula immediately risks fatal refeeding syndrome from sudden electrolyte shifts.
keyClue: SAM with generalised oedema (kwashiorkor) + the suggestion of immediate F-100 is the trap — the correct answer is to stabilise first with F-75.
commonExamTrap: Choosing F-100 because the child is severely malnourished and 'needs more calories' — refeeding syndrome is the lethal complication of too-rapid caloric repletion in SAM.
missedConcept: The WHO SAM protocol has distinct stabilisation (F-75) and rehabilitation (F-100) phases; collapsing them by starting F-100 immediately causes refeeding syndrome.
redFlagReason: Refeeding syndrome from inappropriate high-calorie feeding in SAM causes fatal cardiac arrhythmia and cardiac failure due to acute hypophosphataemia and electrolyte shifts.

clinicalStem: A 12-year-old boy is referred by his school nurse for obesity. His BMI is 32 kg/m² (>99th centile for age). He is asymptomatic. On examination, BP is 136/88 mmHg, there is acanthosis nigricans at the neck and axillae, and waist circumference is 102 cm. Fasting glucose is 6.3 mmol/L and fasting triglycerides are 2.8 mmol/L. HDL is 0.7 mmol/L. He eats fast food daily and is sedentary. Which is the MOST appropriate first-line management approach?
options: Prescribe metformin immediately to treat pre-diabetes and reduce weight
Refer directly to bariatric surgery assessment given BMI >30 in a 12-year-old
Initiate structured lifestyle modification (dietary change + increased physical activity) as the cornerstone of management
Restrict caloric intake to 500 kcal/day and recommend daily vigorous exercise
Prescribe orlistat and reassess in 3 months
explanation: Childhood obesity management is centred on structured lifestyle modification — a combination of dietary changes (reduced sugar, processed food, and energy-dense food), increased physical activity, reduced sedentary time, and family-based behavioural support. This is the cornerstone of all paediatric obesity guidelines and must be attempted before pharmacotherapy. Metformin may be added for confirmed pre-diabetes after lifestyle intervention. Bariatric surgery has very specific criteria in adolescents (typically >14 years with severe obesity and comorbidities). Very-low-calorie diets are contraindicated in growing children.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Metformin is considered for pre-diabetes/type 2 diabetes in adolescents but only after lifestyle modification has been attempted; it is not the first-line step.
Bariatric surgery is not indicated for a 12-year-old; it is reserved for older adolescents (typically ≥14 years) with severe obesity and significant comorbidities after failed lifestyle intervention.
Correct — structured lifestyle modification (diet + physical activity + behavioural change) is the universal first-line management for all children with obesity.
A 500 kcal/day diet is an extreme very-low-calorie diet that is contraindicated in growing children due to risks of growth impairment and nutritional deficiencies.
Orlistat has limited evidence in adolescents, side effects (GI), and is not first-line; it may be considered in older adolescents with severe obesity after lifestyle failure.
clinicalPearl: Childhood obesity management always starts with structured lifestyle modification (diet + activity + behaviour change); pharmacotherapy and surgery are only for selected cases after lifestyle intervention.
keyClue: Acanthosis nigricans, impaired fasting glucose, and hypertriglyceridaemia confirm metabolic syndrome; however, lifestyle modification is still the first management step before any drug.
commonExamTrap: Immediately prescribing metformin for the fasting glucose of 6.3 mmol/L — lifestyle modification must be the first line before any pharmacotherapy in paediatric obesity.
missedConcept: Very-low-calorie diets are dangerous in growing children; lifestyle modification must be structured, moderate, and family-centred — not extreme caloric restriction.

subtopic: Oral iron therapy and dietary advice
clinicalStem: A 14-month-old girl is brought to the clinic for pallor noticed by her mother. She is still predominantly fed on cow's milk (approximately 900 mL/day) with minimal solid foods introduced. On examination she appears pale, is mildly tachycardic at 138 bpm, and has koilonychia. Her haemoglobin is 7.8 g/dL, MCV 62 fL, MCH 18 pg, and serum ferritin is 4 µg/L. She is haemodynamically stable. Which is the MOST appropriate management?
options: Arrange red cell transfusion as the haemoglobin is below 8 g/dL
Start oral iron therapy at 3–6 mg/kg/day of elemental iron and reduce cow's milk intake to <500 mL/day while introducing iron-rich solids
Start IV iron infusion to ensure absorption and bypass gut issues
Prescribe folic acid alone as the macrocytic changes suggest folate deficiency
Reassure the mother that the anaemia will resolve when the child starts eating more solids and no treatment is needed
explanation: This child has nutritional iron deficiency anaemia caused by excessive cow's milk intake (which is low in iron and inhibits iron absorption) displacing iron-rich foods. Treatment is oral iron at 3–6 mg/kg/day of elemental iron for 3 months (2–4 weeks to correct haemoglobin, then a further 2–3 months to replenish stores), combined with dietary advice to reduce cow's milk to <500 mL/day and introduce iron-rich weaning foods. Blood transfusion is reserved for haemodynamically compromised patients or haemoglobin <7 g/dL with symptoms — this child is clinically stable. The anaemia is microcytic, not macrocytic, so folate is not the issue.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Transfusion is reserved for severe symptomatic anaemia (Hb <7 g/dL with haemodynamic compromise); this child is stable and oral iron is the appropriate treatment.
Correct — oral iron at 3–6 mg/kg/day elemental iron plus reduction of cow's milk and introduction of iron-rich foods is the first-line management.
IV iron is not indicated for straightforward nutritional iron deficiency in a child who can tolerate oral medication; it carries risks of anaphylaxis and is not first-line.
The blood film shows microcytic hypochromic anaemia (low MCV, low MCH, low ferritin) consistent with iron deficiency, not folate deficiency (which causes macrocytic anaemia with elevated MCV).
Reassurance without treatment allows ongoing anaemia affecting neurodevelopment and growth; iron deficiency in this age group impairs cognitive development and requires active treatment.
clinicalPearl: Excessive cow's milk (>500 mL/day) is the most common dietary cause of iron deficiency anaemia in toddlers; treat with oral iron AND dietary modification — reduce milk, introduce iron-rich foods.
keyClue: 900 mL/day cow's milk, minimal solids, microcytic hypochromic anaemia, low ferritin = nutritional iron deficiency; oral iron plus dietary change is the treatment.
commonExamTrap: Transfusing for Hb 7.8 g/dL — transfusion is only for severe symptomatic anaemia or Hb <7 g/dL with haemodynamic instability; oral iron is safe and effective here.
missedConcept: Cow's milk is both low in iron and inhibits iron absorption; toddlers consuming >500 mL/day are at high risk for iron deficiency regardless of other dietary intake.

clinicalStem: A 10-year-old boy presents to the ED with a 24-hour history of periumbilical pain that has migrated to the right iliac fossa. He is anorexic and has vomited twice. Temperature is 38.4°C, HR 108 bpm, and RR 20/min. On examination he has maximal tenderness at McBurney's point, rebound tenderness, and voluntary guarding in the right iliac fossa. Rovsing's sign is positive. WBC is 16.2 × 10⁹/L with 88% neutrophils. CRP is 78 mg/L. Which is the MOST appropriate management?
options: Prescribe oral co-amoxiclav for 5 days as an outpatient and reassess in 48 hours
Urgent surgical referral for appendicectomy and start IV fluid resuscitation and analgesia immediately
Perform a diagnostic laparoscopy only after a CT scan confirms the diagnosis
Admit for observation for 24 hours with nil-by-mouth and serial abdominal examinations before any surgical decision
Start IV antibiotics alone as primary treatment for uncomplicated appendicitis in a child
explanation: This boy has classical acute appendicitis with peritoneal signs (rebound tenderness, guarding, positive Rovsing's sign), fever, and a leucocytosis with neutrophilia. In a child with clear clinical features of appendicitis and peritonism, the appropriate management is urgent surgical referral for appendicectomy, alongside IV analgesia, antiemetics, and fluid resuscitation. Delay risks perforation, peritonitis, and sepsis. CT scan is not routinely necessary when clinical diagnosis is secure; US is preferred in children to avoid radiation. Antibiotics alone may be an option for uncomplicated appendicitis, but not when peritoneal signs are present.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Outpatient oral antibiotics are never appropriate for acute appendicitis with peritoneal signs; this delays surgery and risks perforation and faecal peritonitis.
Correct — peritoneal signs with classical clinical features mandate urgent surgical referral and concurrent supportive care (IV fluids, analgesia).
CT scan is not the investigation of first choice in children (radiation risk); ultrasound is preferred, and clinical diagnosis in this case is clear enough to proceed without imaging delay.
24-hour observation without surgical referral when peritoneal signs are already present risks perforation and septic peritonitis — this is too conservative and dangerous.

Antibiotics alone may be considered for simple non-perforated appendicitis without peritonism in selected cases, but this child has peritoneal signs indicating complicated appendicitis requiring surgery.
clinicalPearl: Classical migrated RIF pain + McBurney's tenderness + rebound + positive Rovsing's + raised WBC = acute appendicitis; urgent surgical referral and IV resuscitation take priority — do not wait for imaging to delay surgery.
keyClue: Migration of pain to RIF + McBurney's point tenderness + rebound tenderness + positive Rovsing's sign = acute appendicitis requiring urgent surgical referral.
commonExamTrap: Waiting for CT scan confirmation before referring — in paediatrics, US is preferred over CT, and clear peritoneal signs mandate urgent surgical referral without waiting for imaging.
missedConcept: Peritoneal signs (rebound tenderness, guarding, Rovsing's sign) indicate appendicitis with peritoneal involvement; antibiotics alone or observation without surgical referral increases perforation risk.
redFlagReason: Delayed appendicectomy in a child with peritoneal signs risks perforation, faecal peritonitis, and septic shock — a potentially life-threatening surgical emergency.

clinicalStem: A 16-month-old boy from a low-income household is seen at a primary health clinic with a 3-day history of watery diarrhoea (6 stools/day). He is alert, drinking well, and has no signs of dehydration or systemic illness. His weight is appropriate for age. There is no blood in the stool. Which of the following management steps is specifically recommended by WHO for ALL children aged 6 months to 5 years with acute diarrhoea, in addition to ORS?
options: Empirical oral metronidazole for 5 days to cover Giardia
Oral zinc supplementation 20 mg/day for 10–14 days
Prophylactic oral rehydration salts for 30 days after the episode resolves
Oral co-trimoxazole for 3 days to prevent secondary bacterial infection
Probiotic supplementation for the duration of the diarrhoeal episode
explanation: The WHO and UNICEF recommend zinc supplementation (10 mg/day for infants <6 months; 20 mg/day for children 6 months–5 years) for 10–14 days as a standard adjunct to ORS in all children with acute diarrhoea. Zinc reduces diarrhoea duration by approximately 25%, reduces stool frequency, and decreases the risk of recurrence over the following 2–3 months by supporting intestinal repair and immune function. Empirical antibiotics are not indicated for non-bloody, non-febrile watery diarrhoea, which is predominantly viral.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Metronidazole is not indicated empirically for acute watery diarrhoea without evidence of Giardia or amoebic dysentery; it is not a WHO-recommended standard adjunct.
Correct — WHO recommends oral zinc 20 mg/day for 10–14 days for all children 6 months–5 years with acute diarrhoea, alongside ORS.
Continuing ORS for 30 days after resolution is not a WHO recommendation; ORS is given during active dehydration to replace losses, not prophylactically.
Co-trimoxazole is not indicated for simple watery diarrhoea; empirical antibiotics increase antimicrobial resistance and are reserved for dysentery, cholera, or confirmed bacterial causes.
Probiotics have limited evidence for acute diarrhoea in resource-limited settings and are not part of the standard WHO management protocol for acute diarrhoea.
clinicalPearl: WHO protocol for acute diarrhoea in children: ORS + zinc 20 mg/day for 10–14 days — zinc reduces episode duration by 25% and prevents recurrence for up to 3 months.
keyClue: WHO specifically recommends zinc supplementation for 10–14 days alongside ORS in all children aged 6 months to 5 years with acute diarrhoea, regardless of aetiology.
commonExamTrap: Choosing empirical antibiotics or metronidazole for simple watery diarrhoea — antibiotics are not indicated unless there is blood in stool, high fever, cholera, or confirmed bacterial cause.
missedConcept: Zinc supplementation is a WHO-mandated, evidence-based adjunct to ORS in acute diarrhoea — it is not optional and reduces diarrhoea duration, severity, and recurrence risk.
